Although the selective differentiation of the two enantiotopic linear triple bonds poses a significant challenge, advancements in asymmetric transition metal catalysis and organocatalysis have enabled the efficient synthesis of stereogenic compounds featuring central (both carbon and heteroatom), axial, or planar chirality.


In the field of asymmetric synthesis, the desymmetrization of prochiral diynes has arisen as a challenging yet effective strategy for the rapid and efficient construction of structurally diverse and complex molecules. This review explores the various methodologies (cycloadditions, cycloisomerizations, and C- and X- additions) that enable the enantiotopic discrimination within various symmetric diyne systems, resulting in the generation of central (both carbon and heteroatom), planar, and axial chirality.
item.dc:creator: Anne Boussonnière, Kim Uyen Ly, Anne‐Sophie Castanet
item.dc:title: Enantioselective Desymmetrization of Diynes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Brønsted Acid–Driven C6‐Selective Sulfonylation of Coumarins Promoted by Cu(OTf)2: Synthesis of Coumarin–Sulfone Scaffolds
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description: A Cu(OTf)2‐mediated protocol for the regioselective CH sulfonylation of coumarin derivatives with arylsulfonyl chlorides is reported. This transformation exhibits broad substrate scope and excellent functional‐group tolerance, affording a wide range of C6‐sulfonylated coumarins in moderate to excellent yields. The methodology also extends to heteroaromatic frameworks, such as xanthone, and to bioactive scaffolds exemplified by methoxsalane, enabling selective CH sulfonylation even at unconventional positions. Mechanistic investigations, supported by DFT calculations and Fukui function analysis, reveal that transient protonation of coumarin by in situ–generated triflic acid significantly enhances electrophilic susceptibility at the C6 position, thus overriding the intrinsic C3 reactivity of the neutral scaffold. Computational results favor an electrophilic mechanism over a radical process, as no viable single‐electron donor is available to generate a sulfonyl radical under the reaction conditions. The reaction likely proceeds via electrophilic addition of PhSO2+ to protonated coumarin, followed by a thermodynamically favorable deprotonation step. Overall, this Brønsted acid–assisted sulfonylation provides an efficient, regioselective, and mechanistically well‐supported approach for the functionalization of coumarin and related heteroaromatic systems, thereby expanding the synthetic utility of direct CH sulfonylation reactions.
item.content:encoded: A Cu(OTf)2-mediated protocol for the regioselective CH sulfonylation of coumarin derivatives with arylsulfonyl chlorides is reported. This transformation exhibits broad substrate scope and excellent functional-group tolerance, affording a wide range of C6-sulfonylated coumarins in moderate to excellent yields. The methodology also extends to heteroaromatic frameworks, such as xanthone, and to bioactive scaffolds exemplified by methoxsalane, enabling selective CH sulfonylation even at unconventional positions. Mechanistic investigations, supported by DFT calculations and Fukui function analysis, reveal that transient protonation of coumarin by in situ–generated triflic acid significantly enhances electrophilic susceptibility at the C6 position, thus overriding the intrinsic C3 reactivity of the neutral scaffold. Computational results favor an electrophilic mechanism over a radical process, as no viable single-electron donor is available to generate a sulfonyl radical under the reaction conditions. The reaction likely proceeds via electrophilic addition of PhSO2 + to protonated coumarin, followed by a thermodynamically favorable deprotonation step. Overall, this Brønsted acid–assisted sulfonylation provides an efficient, regioselective, and mechanistically well-supported approach for the functionalization of coumarin and related heteroaromatic systems, thereby expanding the synthetic utility of direct CH sulfonylation reactions.
item.dc:creator: Mukeshkumar Yadav, Pranali Prabhakar Thakur, Mahendra Patil
item.dc:title: Brønsted Acid–Driven C6‐Selective Sulfonylation of Coumarins Promoted by Cu(OTf)2: Synthesis of Coumarin–Sulfone Scaffolds

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chemoselectivity and Enantioselectivity in the Conjugate Reduction of Cinnamate Esters and a Tandem Conjugate Reduction‐Ester Hydrogenation Using Manganese Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An improved synthesis of a manganese catalyst used for hydrogenation of ketones, esters and imines is reported. The catalyst has now been found to effect tandem conjugate reduction‐ester hydrogenations with very low amounts of allylic alcohol by‐products. At low temperatures the reactions give mainly conjugate reduction product without C=O reduction including an example of enantioselective alkene hydrogenation catalyzed by manganese.
An improved synthesis of manganese(I) tricarbonyl complexes of the Phosphino‐Ferrocenyl‐Amino‐Methyl‐Pyridine (PFAMPy) ligand family is reported. A comparison of both the neutral form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)2Br] and the cationic form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br was made for an enantioselective ketone hydrogenation, with both catalysts giving high yields and enantiomer ratios over 98:2. The [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br catalyst was then applied in tandem conjugate reduction‐ester hydrogenation to convert cinnamate esters into aryl propanols. This could be achieved for disubstituted cinnamates with the problematic inseparable allyl alcohol side products almost eliminated below 0.5%. A strategy to prevent CO bond reduction preceding CC reduction, and hence allylic alcohol side products, is to use a tert‐butyl ester and mild conditions for the first few hours of reaction, prior to increasing temperature to promote ester hydrogenation. This approach is needed for trisubstituted cinnamate esters, which otherwise give mixtures. It is possible to carry out just conjugate reductions to saturated esters at lower temperatures without significant ester hydrogenation. Examples of manganese‐catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of alkenes are presented in the form of an enantioselective and chemoselective conjugate reduction of trisubstituted cinnamate esters.
item.content:encoded:
An improved synthesis of a manganese catalyst used for hydrogenation of ketones, esters and imines is reported. The catalyst has now been found to effect tandem conjugate reduction-ester hydrogenations with very low amounts of allylic alcohol by-products. At low temperatures the reactions give mainly conjugate reduction product without C=O reduction including an example of enantioselective alkene hydrogenation catalyzed by manganese.


An improved synthesis of manganese(I) tricarbonyl complexes of the Phosphino-Ferrocenyl-Amino-Methyl-Pyridine (PFAMPy) ligand family is reported. A comparison of both the neutral form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)2Br] and the cationic form [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br was made for an enantioselective ketone hydrogenation, with both catalysts giving high yields and enantiomer ratios over 98:2. The [Mn(PFAMPy)(CO)3]Br catalyst was then applied in tandem conjugate reduction-ester hydrogenation to convert cinnamate esters into aryl propanols. This could be achieved for disubstituted cinnamates with the problematic inseparable allyl alcohol side products almost eliminated below 0.5%. A strategy to prevent CO bond reduction preceding CC reduction, and hence allylic alcohol side products, is to use a tert-butyl ester and mild conditions for the first few hours of reaction, prior to increasing temperature to promote ester hydrogenation. This approach is needed for trisubstituted cinnamate esters, which otherwise give mixtures. It is possible to carry out just conjugate reductions to saturated esters at lower temperatures without significant ester hydrogenation. Examples of manganese-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of alkenes are presented in the form of an enantioselective and chemoselective conjugate reduction of trisubstituted cinnamate esters.
item.dc:creator: José A. Fuentes, Matthew L. Clarke
item.dc:title: Chemoselectivity and Enantioselectivity in the Conjugate Reduction of Cinnamate Esters and a Tandem Conjugate Reduction‐Ester Hydrogenation Using Manganese Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enzymatic Synthesis of Demethylated Sesquiterpenes From 14‐nor‐Farnesyl Pyrophosphate
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The substrate analog 14‐nor‐FPP was synthesised and enzymatically converted with 10 bacterial and fungal sesquiterpene synthases, resulting in the isolation of 12 demethylated sesquiterpenes in yields up to 17%. In most cases the demethyl analogs of the FPP‐derived natural products were obtained, but also different products showing modified reactivities of the substrate analogs were formed.
Previous work has revealed that terpene synthases can efficiently convert non‐natural substrate analogs, which often leads to interesting new structures. In the present study 10 sesquiterpene synthases were used to convert 14‐nor‐FPP into demethyl (C14) sesquiterpenes. The obtained products were isolated and their structures elucidated through NMR spectroscopy. In most cases the demethyl analogs corresponding to the natural products formed from farnesyl pyrophosphate were obtained, but sometimes the structural change in the substrate resulted in a change of reactivity and consequently the formation of structurally deviating products. The absolute configurations were assigned based on biosynthetic considerations and on a comparison of optical rotations to those of the natural C15 analogs.
item.content:encoded:
The substrate analog 14-nor-FPP was synthesised and enzymatically converted with 10 bacterial and fungal sesquiterpene synthases, resulting in the isolation of 12 demethylated sesquiterpenes in yields up to 17%. In most cases the demethyl analogs of the FPP-derived natural products were obtained, but also different products showing modified reactivities of the substrate analogs were formed.


Previous work has revealed that terpene synthases can efficiently convert non-natural substrate analogs, which often leads to interesting new structures. In the present study 10 sesquiterpene synthases were used to convert 14-nor-FPP into demethyl (C14) sesquiterpenes. The obtained products were isolated and their structures elucidated through NMR spectroscopy. In most cases the demethyl analogs corresponding to the natural products formed from farnesyl pyrophosphate were obtained, but sometimes the structural change in the substrate resulted in a change of reactivity and consequently the formation of structurally deviating products. The absolute configurations were assigned based on biosynthetic considerations and on a comparison of optical rotations to those of the natural C15 analogs.
item.dc:creator: Kizerbo A. Taizoumbe, Jeroen S. Dickschat
item.dc:title: Enzymatic Synthesis of Demethylated Sesquiterpenes From 14‐nor‐Farnesyl Pyrophosphate

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Iodine‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–H Amination for the Synthesis of 2‐Arylquinazolines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed for the synthesis of substituted quinazolines in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of 2‐arylquinazolines can be obtained with good yields from readily available starting materials by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, metal‐ and chemical oxidant‐free conditions, and the principles of green chemistry.
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed under mild conditions in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of substituted quinazolines can be obtained with good yields by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, scalability, metal‐free, and chemical oxidant‐free.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient electrochemical C(sp3)–H amination cyclization was developed for the synthesis of substituted quinazolines in the presence of an iodine catalyst. A series of 2-arylquinazolines can be obtained with good yields from readily available starting materials by virtue of the new avenue. The reaction features excellent substrate applicability, metal- and chemical oxidant-free conditions, and the principles of green chemistry.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Facile Synthesis of Carbamates via Base‐Promoted There‐Component Coupling of Aziridines with CO2 and Amines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A base‐promoted, metal‐free three‐component coupling reaction involving aziridines, amines, and CO2 has been developed for the first time. This method enables efficient and highly regioselective synthesis of a diverse range of valuable carbamates in high yields. The protocol benefits from mild reaction conditions, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group tolerance.
Carbamates represent an important class of organic compounds with wide applications. Traditional methods for their synthesis often rely on the use of phosgene and its derivatives. Herein, a base‐promoted three‐component coupling reaction between aziridines, carbon dioxide (CO2), and amines has been successfully developed for the first time, providing an efficient route to a range of structurally diverse and valuable carbamates in high yields with excellent regioselectivities. The metal‐free reaction features mild reaction conditions, high functional group tolerance, and wide substrate scope.
item.content:encoded:
A base-promoted, metal-free three-component coupling reaction involving aziridines, amines, and CO2 has been developed for the first time. This method enables efficient and highly regioselective synthesis of a diverse range of valuable carbamates in high yields. The protocol benefits from mild reaction conditions, broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group tolerance.


Carbamates represent an important class of organic compounds with wide applications. Traditional methods for their synthesis often rely on the use of phosgene and its derivatives. Herein, a base-promoted three-component coupling reaction between aziridines, carbon dioxide (CO2), and amines has been successfully developed for the first time, providing an efficient route to a range of structurally diverse and valuable carbamates in high yields with excellent regioselectivities. The metal-free reaction features mild reaction conditions, high functional group tolerance, and wide substrate scope.
item.dc:creator: Ziyang Li, Li Wei, Zhongrong Xu, Yanwei Ren, Huanfeng Jiang, Chaorong Qi
item.dc:title: Facile Synthesis of Carbamates via Base‐Promoted There‐Component Coupling of Aziridines with CO2 and Amines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Catalytic Hydrogenation of Lignin‐Derived Aromatics With Rhodium Oxide in Water
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The easily available, easy‐to‐handle rhodium(III) oxide, Rh2O3, is shown to be a competent catalyst for arene hydrogenation, including chemoselective reduction of α,β‐unsaturated aryl ketones.
Catalytic arene hydrogenation offers a direct pathway to saturated carbocycles. It can also be exploited to upgrade lignin‐derived aromatics. We have found that one of the most easily available rhodium compounds, rhodium(III) oxide, (Rh2O3), is a highly efficient heterogeneous catalyst for arene hydrogenation in aqueous conditions. A diverse array of arene substrates, including those derived from lignin, underwent smooth conversion into their corresponding saturated products under 5 bar of hydrogen in water. This hydrogenation protocol displayed good compatibility with functional groups and showed tuneable chemoselectivity for α,β‐unsaturated aryl ketones. Given the easy commercial availability of Rh2O3, the method provides a convenient route for synthesising three‐dimensional alicyclic compounds from two‐dimensional aromatic precursors, including those readily accessible from lignin depolymerisation.
item.content:encoded:
The easily available, easy-to-handle rhodium(III) oxide, Rh2O3, is shown to be a competent catalyst for arene hydrogenation, including chemoselective reduction of α,β-unsaturated aryl ketones.


Catalytic arene hydrogenation offers a direct pathway to saturated carbocycles. It can also be exploited to upgrade lignin-derived aromatics. We have found that one of the most easily available rhodium compounds, rhodium(III) oxide, (Rh2O3), is a highly efficient heterogeneous catalyst for arene hydrogenation in aqueous conditions. A diverse array of arene substrates, including those derived from lignin, underwent smooth conversion into their corresponding saturated products under 5 bar of hydrogen in water. This hydrogenation protocol displayed good compatibility with functional groups and showed tuneable chemoselectivity for α,β-unsaturated aryl ketones. Given the easy commercial availability of Rh2O3, the method provides a convenient route for synthesising three-dimensional alicyclic compounds from two-dimensional aromatic precursors, including those readily accessible from lignin depolymerisation.
item.dc:creator: Zhenyu Chen, Leiming Qi, Sydney Williams, Ilkan Calisir, Shamsal Haq, Rasmita Raval, Changzhi Li, Tao Zhang, Jianliang Xiao
item.dc:title: Catalytic Hydrogenation of Lignin‐Derived Aromatics With Rhodium Oxide in Water

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Undirected Primary CH Functionalization of Alkanes via Hydrogen Atom Transfer
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Undirected primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through hydrogen atom transfer. These studies are highlighted in this review, with particular emphasis on the primary selectivity of different kinds of substrates as well as the origin of primary selectivity.
Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) which can produce radical intermediates from alkanes provides a strategy to achieve undirected C(sp3)‐H functionalization. Typically, primary CH bonds are the least reactive in HAT processes, compared to tertiary CH or activated benzylic CH. It is noteworthy that HAT‐mediated primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through the adjustment of various factors such as the catalyst or reagent design. This review summarizes recent advances in undirected primary CH functionalization via the HAT process. These examples will be discussed according to the HAT reagent. Reaction conditions, primary selectivity of various substrates, mechanism, and origin of the primary selectivity will be highlighted.
item.content:encoded:
Undirected primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through hydrogen atom transfer. These studies are highlighted in this review, with particular emphasis on the primary selectivity of different kinds of substrates as well as the origin of primary selectivity.


Hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) which can produce radical intermediates from alkanes provides a strategy to achieve undirected C(sp3)-H functionalization. Typically, primary CH bonds are the least reactive in HAT processes, compared to tertiary CH or activated benzylic CH. It is noteworthy that HAT-mediated primary CH functionalization has recently been realized through the adjustment of various factors such as the catalyst or reagent design. This review summarizes recent advances in undirected primary CH functionalization via the HAT process. These examples will be discussed according to the HAT reagent. Reaction conditions, primary selectivity of various substrates, mechanism, and origin of the primary selectivity will be highlighted.
item.dc:creator: Jie Qin, Jingjing Xia, Yating Zhao, Wujiong Xia
item.dc:title: Undirected Primary CH Functionalization of Alkanes via Hydrogen Atom Transfer

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Unveiling Reaction Pathways in the Alkaline Oxidative Conversion of Guaiacyl Lignin Into Low‐Molecular‐Weight Phenols From β‐Ether Linkages
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Vanillin is an industrial platform chemical produced via the alkaline oxidative degradation of lignin in wood. Our approaches, including reaction tracing with isotopic fingerprinting, indicate that vanillin forms through multiple pathways originating at both phenolic and nonphenolic sites. The by‐products vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone arise primarily from nonphenolic sites.
The oxidative degradation of lignin, a major component of lignocellulose, in alkaline media is important in both academic and applied research, serving as a fundamental approach for lignin analysis and chemical conversion. Although it is well known that the alkaline oxidation commonly yields benzaldehydes, benzoic acids, and acetophenones, the pathways underlying their formation remain insufficiently understood. This study focuses on the β‐ether, the most abundant interunit linkage in lignin, and investigates the formation pathways of vanillin, vanillic acid, and acetoguaiacone from guaiacyl lignin in 4.0 mol/L NaOH aq. at 120°C under O2. By examining the degradation behavior of a lignin model polymer LP composed exclusively of β‐ether, along with various dimeric models, we found that vanillin is mainly generated through a sequential reaction initiated from phenolic terminal units. Another vanillin formation pathway originating from nonphenolic internal units contributed to the overall oxidation process to some extent. Vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone were formed via pathways distinct from that of vanillin, with their formation largely attributed to reactions involving internal units. Further investigation of the degradation behavior of isolated lignin revealed that the contribution of the sequential vanillin formation pathway from phenolic terminals was lower than that observed for LP.
item.content:encoded:
Vanillin is an industrial platform chemical produced via the alkaline oxidative degradation of lignin in wood. Our approaches, including reaction tracing with isotopic fingerprinting, indicate that vanillin forms through multiple pathways originating at both phenolic and nonphenolic sites. The by-products vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone arise primarily from nonphenolic sites.


The oxidative degradation of lignin, a major component of lignocellulose, in alkaline media is important in both academic and applied research, serving as a fundamental approach for lignin analysis and chemical conversion. Although it is well known that the alkaline oxidation commonly yields benzaldehydes, benzoic acids, and acetophenones, the pathways underlying their formation remain insufficiently understood. This study focuses on the β-ether, the most abundant interunit linkage in lignin, and investigates the formation pathways of vanillin, vanillic acid, and acetoguaiacone from guaiacyl lignin in 4.0 mol/L NaOH aq. at 120°C under O2. By examining the degradation behavior of a lignin model polymer LP composed exclusively of β-ether, along with various dimeric models, we found that vanillin is mainly generated through a sequential reaction initiated from phenolic terminal units. Another vanillin formation pathway originating from nonphenolic internal units contributed to the overall oxidation process to some extent. Vanillic acid and acetoguaiacone were formed via pathways distinct from that of vanillin, with their formation largely attributed to reactions involving internal units. Further investigation of the degradation behavior of isolated lignin revealed that the contribution of the sequential vanillin formation pathway from phenolic terminals was lower than that observed for LP.
item.dc:creator: Ayami Ishikawa, Haruka Shimizu, Takashi Hosoya, Hisashi Miyafuji
item.dc:title: Unveiling Reaction Pathways in the Alkaline Oxidative Conversion of Guaiacyl Lignin Into Low‐Molecular‐Weight Phenols From β‐Ether Linkages

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Chiral Isonitriles‐Iron‐Cyclopentadienone Complexes for Enantioselective Transfer Hydrogenation of Ketones
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Inserting isonitriles ligands on a chiral cyclopentadienone complex, upon light activation, chiral catalysts efficiently performed under mild conditions transfer hydrogenation of ketones using isopropanol. The resulting alcohols were obtained in up to 78:22 er, confirming the potential of such iron complexes.
By substituting one CO ligand with an isonitrile, we have developed a novel class of chiral iron cyclopentadienone complexes that are active in transfer hydrogenation under photoactivation. These complexes display high reactivity across a broad range of substrates, achieving enantiomeric ratios of up to 78:22. We explored various strategies for introducing chirality into the iron complex, including the use of chiral cyclopentadienone ligands, chiral isonitrile ligands, and combinations of both. Upon light irradiation, CO dissociation renders the iron center stereogenic, resulting in unique chiral molecules with multiple stereogenic elements. Notably, these iron complexes efficiently reduce ketones under mild conditions using isopropanol as a hydrogen donor, eliminating the need for high hydrogen pressures typically required for such systems. Insights into the role of the chiral cyclopentadienone ligand offer promising directions for designing more selective chiral iron catalysts.
item.content:encoded:
Inserting isonitriles ligands on a chiral cyclopentadienone complex, upon light activation, chiral catalysts efficiently performed under mild conditions transfer hydrogenation of ketones using isopropanol. The resulting alcohols were obtained in up to 78:22 er, confirming the potential of such iron complexes.


By substituting one CO ligand with an isonitrile, we have developed a novel class of chiral iron cyclopentadienone complexes that are active in transfer hydrogenation under photoactivation. These complexes display high reactivity across a broad range of substrates, achieving enantiomeric ratios of up to 78:22. We explored various strategies for introducing chirality into the iron complex, including the use of chiral cyclopentadienone ligands, chiral isonitrile ligands, and combinations of both. Upon light irradiation, CO dissociation renders the iron center stereogenic, resulting in unique chiral molecules with multiple stereogenic elements. Notably, these iron complexes efficiently reduce ketones under mild conditions using isopropanol as a hydrogen donor, eliminating the need for high hydrogen pressures typically required for such systems. Insights into the role of the chiral cyclopentadienone ligand offer promising directions for designing more selective chiral iron catalysts.
item.dc:creator: Gaëtan Quintil, Jacques Pécaut, Ragnar Bjornsson, Amélie Kochem, Adrien Quintard
item.dc:title: Chiral Isonitriles‐Iron‐Cyclopentadienone Complexes for Enantioselective Transfer Hydrogenation of Ketones

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Additives Promote Divergent Reactivity in Photolytic Deoxygenation and Lactonization Reactions of Cobalt Alkoxycarbonyls
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
We report a carbonylation‐homolysis process to access acyl radicals that can undergo decarboxylation or cyclization onto tethered alkenes. In both cases, ligands and additives are shown to influence the outcome to give saturated or unsaturated products, in some cases with isomerization of the initial products. We describe mechanistic experiments providing evidence of cobalt hydrides and the application toward a bioactive lactone natural product.
The formation of lactones via radical‐mediated CC bond formation is a powerful but overlooked method, especially given recent interest in the conversion of alcohols to radical intermediates using photochemistry. Building on our previous work with cobalt porphyrin complexes, a carbonylation‐homolysis process is described for complex unsaturated alcohols and a simple benzylic model system, leading to the production of saturated or unsaturated products. Ligands and additives are demonstrated to promote either reductive or oxidative termination, leading to divergent reactivity in different contexts. The role of cobalt hydride intermediates is also discussed, as well as the application of this strategy toward the synthesis of a lactone‐containing limonoid natural product.
item.content:encoded:
We report a carbonylation-homolysis process to access acyl radicals that can undergo decarboxylation or cyclization onto tethered alkenes. In both cases, ligands and additives are shown to influence the outcome to give saturated or unsaturated products, in some cases with isomerization of the initial products. We describe mechanistic experiments providing evidence of cobalt hydrides and the application toward a bioactive lactone natural product.


The formation of lactones via radical-mediated CC bond formation is a powerful but overlooked method, especially given recent interest in the conversion of alcohols to radical intermediates using photochemistry. Building on our previous work with cobalt porphyrin complexes, a carbonylation-homolysis process is described for complex unsaturated alcohols and a simple benzylic model system, leading to the production of saturated or unsaturated products. Ligands and additives are demonstrated to promote either reductive or oxidative termination, leading to divergent reactivity in different contexts. The role of cobalt hydride intermediates is also discussed, as well as the application of this strategy toward the synthesis of a lactone-containing limonoid natural product.
item.dc:creator: Jacob N. Hackbarth, Dana R. Chambers, Cory T. Ludwig, Tyler K. Brown, David B. C. Martin
item.dc:title: Additives Promote Divergent Reactivity in Photolytic Deoxygenation and Lactonization Reactions of Cobalt Alkoxycarbonyls

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Approaches Toward Arylation Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review provides a systematic analysis of electrochemical arylation reactions. Various reactions such as C‐H arylation, α‐arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O‐arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations has been discussed with their mechanistic insights.
Electrochemical arylation has emerged as a sustainable and efficient approach for forming CC and Cheteroatom bonds. These reactions provide a green alternative to conventional arylation methods that often rely on harsh reaction conditions. The present review categorizes electrochemical arylation reactions into key types, such as C–H arylation, α‐arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O‐arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations. Each class highlights distinct mechanistic features and substrate scopes, demonstrating the versatility of electrochemical techniques in constructing diverse arylated frameworks. Overall, this review provides a comprehensive understanding of how electrochemical strategies are revolutionizing arylation chemistry and paving the way toward more sustainable organic synthesis.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides a systematic analysis of electrochemical arylation reactions. Various reactions such as C-H arylation, α-arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O-arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations has been discussed with their mechanistic insights.


Electrochemical arylation has emerged as a sustainable and efficient approach for forming CC and Cheteroatom bonds. These reactions provide a green alternative to conventional arylation methods that often rely on harsh reaction conditions. The present review categorizes electrochemical arylation reactions into key types, such as C–H arylation, α-arylation, oxidative arylation, reductive arylation, decarboxylative arylation, O-arylation, and other miscellaneous transformations. Each class highlights distinct mechanistic features and substrate scopes, demonstrating the versatility of electrochemical techniques in constructing diverse arylated frameworks. Overall, this review provides a comprehensive understanding of how electrochemical strategies are revolutionizing arylation chemistry and paving the way toward more sustainable organic synthesis.

item.dc:title: Electrochemical Approaches Toward Arylation Reactions

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: EDA Complex–Mediated C(sp3)–H Cross‐Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Synthesis of Noncanonical α,β‐Diamino Acids
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Access to nonproteinogenic α,β‐diamino acids from simple feedstock precursors is of great interest to generate medicinal small molecule libraries. We report a regioselective photoinduced C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross‐dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N‐arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex of electron‐deficient aryl halides to generate reactive aryl radicals capable of efficient hydrogen‐atom transfer (HAT) processes.
Nonproteinogenic α,β‐diamino acids are key motifs in bioactive compounds. We report a photoinduced cross‐dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N‐arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex that enables regioselective C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross‐coupling, providing access to non‐canonical α,β‐diamino acids from simple precursors.
item.content:encoded:
Access to nonproteinogenic α,β-diamino acids from simple feedstock precursors is of great interest to generate medicinal small molecule libraries. We report a regioselective photoinduced C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N-arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex of electron-deficient aryl halides to generate reactive aryl radicals capable of efficient hydrogen-atom transfer (HAT) processes.


Nonproteinogenic α,β-diamino acids are key motifs in bioactive compounds. We report a photoinduced cross-dehydrogenative coupling (CDC) of N-arylglycine derivatives with amides. This reaction proceeds via an electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex that enables regioselective C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross-coupling, providing access to non-canonical α,β-diamino acids from simple precursors.

item.dc:title: EDA Complex–Mediated C(sp3)–H Cross‐Dehydrogenative Coupling Enables Synthesis of Noncanonical α,β‐Diamino Acids

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Sulfur Heterocycles by Palladium‐Catalyzed Cyclization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
In spite of the well‐known palladium thiophilicity, sulfur‐containing acyclic substrates may efficiently undergo several different kinds of palladium‐catalyzed cyclization processes leading to highly important sulfur heterocycles in one synthetic step. Advances in this challenging and stimulating field of research are presented in this review.
The minireview is aimed at giving an overview on palladium‐catalyzed cyclization methods for the synthesis of important sulfur heterocycles, including thiophenes, benzothiophenes, benzothiazoles, and sulfur‐containing polycyclic systems. The minireview is organized into several sections based on the kind of reactivity leading to the sulfur heterocycle, namely: Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of aryl and vinyl halide derivatives; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization by C—H activation; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of olefinic substrates; Pd‐catalyzed cyclization of acetylenic substrates; Pd‐catalyzed carbonylative cyclization; cyclization by Pd‐catalyzed ring opening—ring closure. The review covers Pd‐catalyzed S‐cyclizations as well as transformations in which the sulfur atom lies within the chain undergoing annulation.
item.content:encoded:
In spite of the well-known palladium thiophilicity, sulfur-containing acyclic substrates may efficiently undergo several different kinds of palladium-catalyzed cyclization processes leading to highly important sulfur heterocycles in one synthetic step. Advances in this challenging and stimulating field of research are presented in this review.


The minireview is aimed at giving an overview on palladium-catalyzed cyclization methods for the synthesis of important sulfur heterocycles, including thiophenes, benzothiophenes, benzothiazoles, and sulfur-containing polycyclic systems. The minireview is organized into several sections based on the kind of reactivity leading to the sulfur heterocycle, namely: Pd-catalyzed cyclization of aryl and vinyl halide derivatives; Pd-catalyzed cyclization by C—H activation; Pd-catalyzed cyclization of olefinic substrates; Pd-catalyzed cyclization of acetylenic substrates; Pd-catalyzed carbonylative cyclization; cyclization by Pd-catalyzed ring opening—ring closure. The review covers Pd-catalyzed S-cyclizations as well as transformations in which the sulfur atom lies within the chain undergoing annulation.
item.dc:creator: Bartolo Gabriele
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Sulfur Heterocycles by Palladium‐Catalyzed Cyclization

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Transfer Hydrogenation of Organic Azides to Amines by Ammonia Borane Complex Under Mild Conditions Catalyzed by Reusable Au Nanoparticles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Organic azides are readily reduced to amines under mild conditions by ammonia borane complex, catalyzed by a low loading level of reusable Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2.
We present the first protocol for the chemoselective reduction of organic azides to amines by ammonia borane complex. In the presence of a low catalytic amount of Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2 (0.5 mol%), alkyl and aryl azides are readily reduced to amines by NH3BH3 (1 M equivalent) within 15–30 min at room temperature in a protic solvent such as ethanol or methanol. The reduction of the azide functionality occurs faster relative to coexisting nitro group, while other formally reducible functionalities such as cyano, ester or halides remain intact. The catalyst can be easily recycled and reused equally well at least 3 consecutive times.
item.content:encoded:
Organic azides are readily reduced to amines under mild conditions by ammonia borane complex, catalyzed by a low loading level of reusable Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2.


We present the first protocol for the chemoselective reduction of organic azides to amines by ammonia borane complex. In the presence of a low catalytic amount of Au nanoparticles supported on TiO2 (0.5 mol%), alkyl and aryl azides are readily reduced to amines by NH3BH3 (1 M equivalent) within 15–30 min at room temperature in a protic solvent such as ethanol or methanol. The reduction of the azide functionality occurs faster relative to coexisting nitro group, while other formally reducible functionalities such as cyano, ester or halides remain intact. The catalyst can be easily recycled and reused equally well at least 3 consecutive times.
item.dc:creator: Elisavet‐Maria Zantioti‐Chatzouda, Dimitra Malliotaki, Ioannis Zachilas, Andreas‐Drakos Kokkinos, Orestis Agathocleous, Manolis Stratakis
item.dc:title: Transfer Hydrogenation of Organic Azides to Amines by Ammonia Borane Complex Under Mild Conditions Catalyzed by Reusable Au Nanoparticles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Progress in Transition‐Metal‐Catalyzed C–H Functionalization of 2‐Arylthiazoles and 2‐Arylbenzothiazoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition‐metal‐catalyzed regioselective C–H functionalization of 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles, covering a broad spectrum of C–C and C–X bond‐forming transformations. Key catalytic strategies, mechanistic insights, and emerging methodologies are highlighted, providing guidance for the selective diversification of sulfur‐containing heteroarenes in medicinal and materials chemistry.
Heterocyclic compounds play a central role in modern organic chemistry, underpinning the structural frameworks of numerous bioactive molecules and functional materials. In particular, 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles have emerged as privileged sulfur‐containing heteroarenes, valued for their diverse biological activities and favorable electronic properties. Their rigid, π‐conjugated architectures enable broad applications ranging from medicinal chemistry to optoelectronic materials. Consequently, efficient methods for their synthesis and selective structural modification are of considerable importance. While classical synthetic routes and cross‐coupling strategies provide access to these scaffolds, their reliance on prefunctionalized substrates and multistep sequences limits synthetic efficiency and late‐stage diversification. In response, transition‐metal‐catalyzed C–H functionalization has become a powerful alternative, allowing direct and atom‐economical modification of heteroarenes. Recent advances have addressed the inherent challenges of sulfur‐containing substrates, enabling precise regioselective and regiodivergent transformations. This review summarizes recent progress in transition‐metal‐catalyzed C–H functionalization of 2‐arylthiazoles and 2‐arylbenzothiazoles, highlighting key reaction modes, mechanistic insights, and representative applications.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in transition-metal-catalyzed regioselective C–H functionalization of 2-arylthiazoles and 2-arylbenzothiazoles, covering a broad spectrum of C–C and C–X bond-forming transformations. Key catalytic strategies, mechanistic insights, and emerging methodologies are highlighted, providing guidance for the selective diversification of sulfur-containing heteroarenes in medicinal and materials chemistry.


Heterocyclic compounds play a central role in modern organic chemistry, underpinning the structural frameworks of numerous bioactive molecules and functional materials. In particular, 2-arylthiazoles and 2-arylbenzothiazoles have emerged as privileged sulfur-containing heteroarenes, valued for their diverse biological activities and favorable electronic properties. Their rigid, π-conjugated architectures enable broad applications ranging from medicinal chemistry to optoelectronic materials. Consequently, efficient methods for their synthesis and selective structural modification are of considerable importance. While classical synthetic routes and cross-coupling strategies provide access to these scaffolds, their reliance on prefunctionalized substrates and multistep sequences limits synthetic efficiency and late-stage diversification. In response, transition-metal-catalyzed C–H functionalization has become a powerful alternative, allowing direct and atom-economical modification of heteroarenes. Recent advances have addressed the inherent challenges of sulfur-containing substrates, enabling precise regioselective and regiodivergent transformations. This review summarizes recent progress in transition-metal-catalyzed C–H functionalization of 2-arylthiazoles and 2-arylbenzothiazoles, highlighting key reaction modes, mechanistic insights, and representative applications.
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Lu, Mei Chen, Shuangliang Zhao, Zhuan Zhang
item.dc:title: Progress in Transition‐Metal‐Catalyzed C–H Functionalization of 2‐Arylthiazoles and 2‐Arylbenzothiazoles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Functionalized 10(2,4,6‐Triarylphenyl)‐10H‐phenothiazines Synthesized Without Metal Catalysts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
New sterically highly congested triaryl phenyl phenothiazines are obtained via Zimmermann–Fischer condensation and structurally investigated by single crystal X‐ray analysis.
A new synthetic approach to functionalized N‐(2,4,6‐triarylphenyl) phenothiazines is presented. The methodology is based on a modified Fischer–Zimmermann condensation of triaryl pyrylium salts with aryl acetates, specifically sodium β‐N‐phenothiazine acetate, and affords sterically highly congested compounds. Single‐crystal X‐ray analyses of several of these compounds reveal that the pronounced steric hindrance results in an almost perpendicular orientation between the triaryl phenyl moiety ring and the phenothiazine core, accompanied by a significantly reduced folding angle of the latter. These structural arrangements also correlate with the low oxidation potential of the compounds.
item.content:encoded:
New sterically highly congested triaryl phenyl phenothiazines are obtained via Zimmermann–Fischer condensation and structurally investigated by single crystal X-ray analysis.


A new synthetic approach to functionalized N-(2,4,6-triarylphenyl) phenothiazines is presented. The methodology is based on a modified Fischer–Zimmermann condensation of triaryl pyrylium salts with aryl acetates, specifically sodium β-N-phenothiazine acetate, and affords sterically highly congested compounds. Single-crystal X-ray analyses of several of these compounds reveal that the pronounced steric hindrance results in an almost perpendicular orientation between the triaryl phenyl moiety ring and the phenothiazine core, accompanied by a significantly reduced folding angle of the latter. These structural arrangements also correlate with the low oxidation potential of the compounds.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Rebecca Wentzke, Ute Müller, Anh Stummer, Andreas Schneider, Gregor Schnakenburg, Pierre Pascal Kachel, Inke Siewert, Sigurd Höger
item.dc:title: Functionalized 10(2,4,6‐Triarylphenyl)‐10H‐phenothiazines Synthesized Without Metal Catalysts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Brønsted Acid Mediated Aglycon Swapping Editing Reaction (ASER)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A new aglycon editing reaction by tuning stereoelectronic effects is reported and it is termed as aglycon swapping editing reaction (ASER).
Chemical synthesis of glycoconjugates is a formidable challenge due to the complex interplay of factors affecting glycoside formation. In Nature, glycosidases guide the precise stereo‐ and regio‐selective swapping of aglycons by harnessing the spatial proximity of reactive partners. However, replicating this control in chemical synthesis has proven difficult. Here, we introduce the aglycon swapping editing reaction (ASER) as a novel approach to the glycan editing. Our design features a strategically cleavable trisaccharide yielding a nonreactive 1,6‐anhydro sugar by extrusion of a highly electrophilic intermediate. Hence, produced intermediate is primed for nucleophilic attack enabling the formation of a diverse array of glycoconjugates such as O‐, N‐, S‐, and C‐glycosides. Our findings reveal ASER as a versatile way to access structurally varied glycoconjugates broadening the toolkit for glycan syntheses.
item.content:encoded:
A new aglycon editing reaction by tuning stereoelectronic effects is reported and it is termed as aglycon swapping editing reaction (ASER).


Chemical synthesis of glycoconjugates is a formidable challenge due to the complex interplay of factors affecting glycoside formation. In Nature, glycosidases guide the precise stereo- and regio-selective swapping of aglycons by harnessing the spatial proximity of reactive partners. However, replicating this control in chemical synthesis has proven difficult. Here, we introduce the aglycon swapping editing reaction (ASER) as a novel approach to the glycan editing. Our design features a strategically cleavable trisaccharide yielding a nonreactive 1,6-anhydro sugar by extrusion of a highly electrophilic intermediate. Hence, produced intermediate is primed for nucleophilic attack enabling the formation of a diverse array of glycoconjugates such as O-, N-, S-, and C-glycosides. Our findings reveal ASER as a versatile way to access structurally varied glycoconjugates broadening the toolkit for glycan syntheses.
item.dc:creator: Sumit Sen, Suman Kundu, Srinivas Hotha
item.dc:title: Brønsted Acid Mediated Aglycon Swapping Editing Reaction (ASER)

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Total Synthesis of the Naturally Occurring Amaryllidaceae Alkaloid (+)‐Zephycitrine
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The first asymmetric total syntheses of the naturally occurring Amaryllidaceae alkaloid (+)‐zephycitrine has been achieved. This efficient 11‐step route establishes, through a Mitsunobu reaction of an enantioenriched allylic alcohol, a microwave‐assisted intramolecular carbonyl‐ene reaction followed by a late‐stage Pictet–Spengler cyclization and was completed in an overall yield of 8.3%.
The first catalytic asymmetric total synthesis of the naturally occurring Amaryllidaceae alkaloid, (+)‐zephycitrine (1a), has been accomplished. This efficient 11‐step route (8.3% overall yield) establishes the key stereocenters using an organocatalytic Corey–Itsuno reduction. The construction of the complex core structure, featuring three contiguous stereocenters, was subsequently realized via a Mitsunobu reaction and a microwave‐assisted intramolecular carbonyl‐ene reaction. The synthesis was completed with a final Pictet–Spengler cyclization. This concise approach, employing a sequence of classical and robust reactions, provides a versatile and efficient platform for accessing pro‐drug derivatives and other members of the Amaryllidaceae alkaloid family.
item.content:encoded:
The first asymmetric total syntheses of the naturally occurring Amaryllidaceae alkaloid (+)-zephycitrine has been achieved. This efficient 11-step route establishes, through a Mitsunobu reaction of an enantioenriched allylic alcohol, a microwave-assisted intramolecular carbonyl-ene reaction followed by a late-stage Pictet–Spengler cyclization and was completed in an overall yield of 8.3%.


The first catalytic asymmetric total synthesis of the naturally occurring Amaryllidaceae alkaloid, (+)-zephycitrine (1a), has been accomplished. This efficient 11-step route (8.3% overall yield) establishes the key stereocenters using an organocatalytic Corey–Itsuno reduction. The construction of the complex core structure, featuring three contiguous stereocenters, was subsequently realized via a Mitsunobu reaction and a microwave-assisted intramolecular carbonyl-ene reaction. The synthesis was completed with a final Pictet–Spengler cyclization. This concise approach, employing a sequence of classical and robust reactions, provides a versatile and efficient platform for accessing pro-drug derivatives and other members of the Amaryllidaceae alkaloid family.
item.dc:creator: Abhishek Mondal, Ayan Mondal, Satadru Dutta, Tiyasa Roy, Alakesh Bisai
item.dc:title: Total Synthesis of the Naturally Occurring Amaryllidaceae Alkaloid (+)‐Zephycitrine

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Saccharin‐Based Reagents and Catalysts: Expanding the Toolbox of Organic Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This minireview summarizes recent advances in saccharin‐based reagents and catalysts, highlighting their versatility in molecular functionalization and ion‐pair catalysis. These developments underscore saccharin's emergence as a powerful scaffold that expands the synthetic toolbox of modern organic chemistry.
Saccharin, known as the earliest artificial sweetener, has undergone a profound chemical redefinition from a food additive to a versatile molecular platform in modern organic synthesis. Its rigid benzobenzisothiazolone framework, electron‐deficient sulfur (VI) center, and stabilized conjugate base confer unique acidity, leaving‐group capacity, and tunable reactivity. These intrinsic properties have been leveraged to develop a rapidly expanding repertoire of saccharin‐derived reagents and catalytic systems. Recent advances demonstrate saccharin as a privileged synthon and functional‐group transfer platform enabling amination, trifluoromethylthiolation, trifluoromethylselenylation, disulfuration, and acylation under mild and sustainable conditions. Saccharin‐based ion‐pair catalysts have further enabled halide‐ and metal‐free CO2 activation, while hypervalent iodine (III) reagents and saccharin‐derived zwitterions have opened new avenues for photocatalysis and chemodivergent cross‐couplings. In general, these developments highlight saccharin's emerging role in late‐stage functionalization, medicinal chemistry, and complex molecule construction. This review summarizes recent mechanistic and methodological advances of saccharin‐derived compounds as reagents and catalysts, and outlines future opportunities in chiral reagent design, catalytic asymmetric transformations, and scalable applications.
item.content:encoded:
This minireview summarizes recent advances in saccharin-based reagents and catalysts, highlighting their versatility in molecular functionalization and ion-pair catalysis. These developments underscore saccharin's emergence as a powerful scaffold that expands the synthetic toolbox of modern organic chemistry.


Saccharin, known as the earliest artificial sweetener, has undergone a profound chemical redefinition from a food additive to a versatile molecular platform in modern organic synthesis. Its rigid benzobenzisothiazolone framework, electron-deficient sulfur (VI) center, and stabilized conjugate base confer unique acidity, leaving-group capacity, and tunable reactivity. These intrinsic properties have been leveraged to develop a rapidly expanding repertoire of saccharin-derived reagents and catalytic systems. Recent advances demonstrate saccharin as a privileged synthon and functional-group transfer platform enabling amination, trifluoromethylthiolation, trifluoromethylselenylation, disulfuration, and acylation under mild and sustainable conditions. Saccharin-based ion-pair catalysts have further enabled halide- and metal-free CO2 activation, while hypervalent iodine (III) reagents and saccharin-derived zwitterions have opened new avenues for photocatalysis and chemodivergent cross-couplings. In general, these developments highlight saccharin's emerging role in late-stage functionalization, medicinal chemistry, and complex molecule construction. This review summarizes recent mechanistic and methodological advances of saccharin-derived compounds as reagents and catalysts, and outlines future opportunities in chiral reagent design, catalytic asymmetric transformations, and scalable applications.
item.dc:creator: Junqiang Wei, Li Yue, Shuo Zhang, Lulu Liu, Longji Li, Yang’en You
item.dc:title: Saccharin‐Based Reagents and Catalysts: Expanding the Toolbox of Organic Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Heteroaryl‐Substituted Polyfluorobiaryls Synthesis via Palladium‐Catalyzed Double CH Bond Functionalization Using Palladium 1,4‐Migration Associated to Direct Arylation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Fluoro substituents on a benzene ring linked to a 2‐bromoaryl group enables the selective activation of one of its CH bonds via Pd‐1,4‐migration. Then, Pd‐catalyzed direct coupling with heteroarenes produces C2’‐heteroarylated 1,1′‐biaryls. In this reaction, the new C–C bond arises from the functionalization of two CH bonds.
The functionalization of specific CH bonds of arenes is a topic currently receiving significant attention, as it may enable the preparation of complex molecules in fewer steps. We found that using 2‐bromo‐1,1′‐biaryls bearing fluoro substituents at the 2′, 3′, 4′, and/or 5′ positions, in the presence of heteroarenes and a palladium catalyst, C2′‐heteroarylated 1,1′‐biaryls derivatives are formed. In the course of this coupling reaction, the created CC bond arises from the functionalization of two CH bonds, with the bromide C2‐substituent behaving as a traceless directing group. This method allows to activate regioselectively of the CH bond at the C2′‐position of the polyfluorobenzene ring, even in the presence of a more reactive CH bond on this ring. This approach is synthetically attractive for the heteroarylation of the C2′‐position of 2‐bromo‐1,1′‐biphenyls, since preparing 2‐bromo‐1,1′‐biphenyls with fluoro substituents on the same ring as the bromo substituent is challenging. Moreover, this procedure employs an easily available air‐stable catalyst and an inexpensive base.
item.content:encoded:
Fluoro substituents on a benzene ring linked to a 2-bromoaryl group enables the selective activation of one of its CH bonds via Pd-1,4-migration. Then, Pd-catalyzed direct coupling with heteroarenes produces C2’-heteroarylated 1,1′-biaryls. In this reaction, the new C–C bond arises from the functionalization of two CH bonds.


The functionalization of specific CH bonds of arenes is a topic currently receiving significant attention, as it may enable the preparation of complex molecules in fewer steps. We found that using 2-bromo-1,1′-biaryls bearing fluoro substituents at the 2′, 3′, 4′, and/or 5′ positions, in the presence of heteroarenes and a palladium catalyst, C2′-heteroarylated 1,1′-biaryls derivatives are formed. In the course of this coupling reaction, the created CC bond arises from the functionalization of two CH bonds, with the bromide C2-substituent behaving as a traceless directing group. This method allows to activate regioselectively of the CH bond at the C2′-position of the polyfluorobenzene ring, even in the presence of a more reactive CH bond on this ring. This approach is synthetically attractive for the heteroarylation of the C2′-position of 2-bromo-1,1′-biphenyls, since preparing 2-bromo-1,1′-biphenyls with fluoro substituents on the same ring as the bromo substituent is challenging. Moreover, this procedure employs an easily available air-stable catalyst and an inexpensive base.
item.dc:creator: Norman Le Floch, Thierry Roisnel, Henri Doucet
item.dc:title: Heteroaryl‐Substituted Polyfluorobiaryls Synthesis via Palladium‐Catalyzed Double CH Bond Functionalization Using Palladium 1,4‐Migration Associated to Direct Arylation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Hypervalent Iodine‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfones Using Organozinc Pivalates and Sulfinate Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A general protocol for sulfone synthesis via sulfonyl‐containing hypervalent iodine reagents and organozinc pivalates is described. The method is practical, provides a broad scope of sulfone in moderate to high yield. bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane‐sulfones are also disclosed and a one‐pot synthesis of Diels–Alder adduct is presented.
Sulfones are key motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and functional materials, as well as versatile intermediates in synthesis. We report a general and practical protocol for sulfone synthesis that combines the group‐transfer capability of hypervalent iodine reagents (HIRs)—particularly in situ‐generated sulfonyl‐containing HIRs—with organozinc pivalates. The method delivers a broad range of sulfones in high yields under mild conditions, exhibiting excellent functional group tolerance. Importantly, the method enables efficient sulfonylation using sodium bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane sulfinate (BCP–SO2Na), providing streamlined access to functionalized BCP‐sulfones. The protocol further enables in situ sulfinate generation and one‐pot transformations. These findings expand the scope of HIR‐enabled umpolung reactivity and offer a practical, modular and operationally simple platform for accessing sulfones with broad synthetic and medicinally relevance.
item.content:encoded:
A general protocol for sulfone synthesis via sulfonyl-containing hypervalent iodine reagents and organozinc pivalates is described. The method is practical, provides a broad scope of sulfone in moderate to high yield. bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane-sulfones are also disclosed and a one-pot synthesis of Diels–Alder adduct is presented.


Sulfones are key motifs in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and functional materials, as well as versatile intermediates in synthesis. We report a general and practical protocol for sulfone synthesis that combines the group-transfer capability of hypervalent iodine reagents (HIRs)—particularly in situ-generated sulfonyl-containing HIRs—with organozinc pivalates. The method delivers a broad range of sulfones in high yields under mild conditions, exhibiting excellent functional group tolerance. Importantly, the method enables efficient sulfonylation using sodium bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane sulfinate (BCP–SO2Na), providing streamlined access to functionalized BCP-sulfones. The protocol further enables in situ sulfinate generation and one-pot transformations. These findings expand the scope of HIR-enabled umpolung reactivity and offer a practical, modular and operationally simple platform for accessing sulfones with broad synthetic and medicinally relevance.
item.dc:creator: José C. Cunha, Gabriel G. Rocha, Fábio M. F. Santos, Stefan Bräse, M. Manuel B. Marques
item.dc:title: Hypervalent Iodine‐Mediated Synthesis of Sulfones Using Organozinc Pivalates and Sulfinate Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isocyanide‐Based Three‐Component Reaction for Construction of Spiro and Fused Furan Frameworks
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A practical synthetic protocol for diverse spiro and fused furan frameworks was developed by isocyanide‐based three‐component reactions. The three‐component reaction of alkyl isocyanides, dialkyl but‐2‐ynedioates, and 3‐(o‐hydroxyphenacylidene)oxindoles or 2‐(indol‐3‐yl)indene‐1,3‐diones afforded special spiro[benzofuran‐3,2′‐furans] and indeno[2,1‐b]oxeto[2,3‐c]furans in good yields and with high diastereoselectivity.
A practical synthetic protocol for diverse spiro and fused furan frameworks was developed by isocyanide‐based three‐component reactions. The three‐component reaction of alkyl isocyanides, dialkyl but‐2‐ynedioates, and 3‐(o‐hydroxyphenacylidene)oxindoles or 2‐(indol‐3‐yl)indene‐1,3‐diones afforded special spiro[benzofuran‐3,2′‐furans] and indeno[2,1‐b]oxeto[2,3‐c]furans in good yields and with high diastereoselectivity. The single crystal structure determination showed that the unique polycyclic compounds with spiro and fused furan frameworks were efficiently formed.
item.content:encoded:

The three-component reaction of alkyl isocyanides, dialkyl but-2-ynedioates, and 3-(o-hydroxyphenacylidene)oxindoles or 2-(indol-3-yl)indene-1,3-diones afforded special spiro[benzofuran-3,2′-furans] and indeno[2,1-b]oxeto[2,3-c]furans in good yields and with high diastereoselectivity.


A practical synthetic protocol for diverse spiro and fused furan frameworks was developed by isocyanide-based three-component reactions. The three-component reaction of alkyl isocyanides, dialkyl but-2-ynedioates, and 3-(o-hydroxyphenacylidene)oxindoles or 2-(indol-3-yl)indene-1,3-diones afforded special spiro[benzofuran-3,2′-furans] and indeno[2,1-b]oxeto[2,3-c]furans in good yields and with high diastereoselectivity. The single crystal structure determination showed that the unique polycyclic compounds with spiro and fused furan frameworks were efficiently formed.

item.dc:title: Isocyanide‐Based Three‐Component Reaction for Construction of Spiro and Fused Furan Frameworks

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Substrate‐Controlled Regioselective Dibenzylation Reaction of 1,3‐Dicarbonyl Compounds: Access to α,α‐Dibenzylated and O‐Benzylated‐α‐Benzylated Products
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
α,α‐Dibenzylated and O‐benzylated‐α‐benzylated products are easily constructed via a KOH‐mediated substrate‐controlled regioselective dibenzylation reaction of 1,3‐dicarbonyl compounds with benzyl bromides.
A substrate‐controlled method for regioselective dibenzylation reaction of 1,3‐dicarbonyl compounds has been developed. The protocol employs readily available 1,3‐dicarbonyl compounds and benzyl bromides mediated by KOH at room temperature, which enables the divergent synthesis of α,α‐dibenzylated products and O‐benzylated‐α‐benzylated products with wide substrate scope and good functional group compatibility. Gram‐scale synthesis and further functionalization reaction demonstrate the potential practicability of this method in organic synthesis and drug development.
item.content:encoded:
α,α-Dibenzylated and O-benzylated-α-benzylated products are easily constructed via a KOH-mediated substrate-controlled regioselective dibenzylation reaction of 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds with benzyl bromides.


A substrate-controlled method for regioselective dibenzylation reaction of 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds has been developed. The protocol employs readily available 1,3-dicarbonyl compounds and benzyl bromides mediated by KOH at room temperature, which enables the divergent synthesis of α,α-dibenzylated products and O-benzylated-α-benzylated products with wide substrate scope and good functional group compatibility. Gram-scale synthesis and further functionalization reaction demonstrate the potential practicability of this method in organic synthesis and drug development.

item.dc:title: Substrate‐Controlled Regioselective Dibenzylation Reaction of 1,3‐Dicarbonyl Compounds: Access to α,α‐Dibenzylated and O‐Benzylated‐α‐Benzylated Products

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Carboamination Tandem Cyclization of Propargyl Alcohols for Assembly of 2,3‐Disubstituted Quinolines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A nickel‐catalyzed three‐component carboamination tandem cyclization strategy has been developed for the synthesis of 2,3‐disubstituted quinolines from propargyl alcohols, organoboronic acids, and anthranils.
A nickel‐catalyzed three‐component carboamination tandem cyclization strategy has been developed for the synthesis of 2,3‐disubstituted quinolines from propargyl alcohols, organoboronic acids, and anthranils. A novel α,β‐unsaturated imine intermediate plays a pivotal role in this system, undergoing base‐mediated annulation to assemble the quinoline core structure. This protocol exhibits good functional group tolerance and can be applied to the synthesis of 2,3‐unsymmetrically disubstituted quinolines and 2‐vinyl quinolines.
item.content:encoded:
A nickel-catalyzed three-component carboamination tandem cyclization strategy has been developed for the synthesis of 2,3-disubstituted quinolines from propargyl alcohols, organoboronic acids, and anthranils.


A nickel-catalyzed three-component carboamination tandem cyclization strategy has been developed for the synthesis of 2,3-disubstituted quinolines from propargyl alcohols, organoboronic acids, and anthranils. A novel α,β-unsaturated imine intermediate plays a pivotal role in this system, undergoing base-mediated annulation to assemble the quinoline core structure. This protocol exhibits good functional group tolerance and can be applied to the synthesis of 2,3-unsymmetrically disubstituted quinolines and 2-vinyl quinolines.

item.dc:title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Three‐Component Carboamination Tandem Cyclization of Propargyl Alcohols for Assembly of 2,3‐Disubstituted Quinolines

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemically Generated Carbanions in Organic Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Electrochemistry revolutionizes carbanion chemistry. A broad range of electrochemical protocols has been developed for carbanion generation, circumventing conventional Schlenk techniques and tedious procedures. Recent progress in the field has been concluded, and the impressive strategies generating carbanions from double bonds, carbon‐heteroatom bonds, and CH bonds are discussed in this review.
Carbanion is one of the most classical intermediates in organic transformations. Traditionally, generating these reactive species requires stoichiometric amounts of organic bases or reactive metals, often involving Schlenk techniques and tedious procedures. To address these issues, a broad range of electrochemical protocols has been developed for carbanion generation via an electrochemical reduction pathway. Despite the tremendous development, the potential of electrochemically generated carbanions remains largely overlooked and underexplored. Herein, we reviewed recent progress in the field, and the impressive strategies generating carbanions from double bonds, carbon‐heteroatom bonds, and CH bonds are discussed. Furthermore, the challenges existing in the present reports and future directions were also covered. It is noteworthy that the review highlights chemistry from the last 5 years and is not intended to be exhaustive.
item.content:encoded:
Electrochemistry revolutionizes carbanion chemistry. A broad range of electrochemical protocols has been developed for carbanion generation, circumventing conventional Schlenk techniques and tedious procedures. Recent progress in the field has been concluded, and the impressive strategies generating carbanions from double bonds, carbon-heteroatom bonds, and CH bonds are discussed in this review.


Carbanion is one of the most classical intermediates in organic transformations. Traditionally, generating these reactive species requires stoichiometric amounts of organic bases or reactive metals, often involving Schlenk techniques and tedious procedures. To address these issues, a broad range of electrochemical protocols has been developed for carbanion generation via an electrochemical reduction pathway. Despite the tremendous development, the potential of electrochemically generated carbanions remains largely overlooked and underexplored. Herein, we reviewed recent progress in the field, and the impressive strategies generating carbanions from double bonds, carbon-heteroatom bonds, and CH bonds are discussed. Furthermore, the challenges existing in the present reports and future directions were also covered. It is noteworthy that the review highlights chemistry from the last 5 years and is not intended to be exhaustive.

item.dc:title: Electrochemically Generated Carbanions in Organic Synthesis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Z‐Styrenyl‐1,2‐bis Dithiocarbamate via Metal‐Free Activation of Alkynyl Bromide
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Alkynyl bromide undergoes metal‐free activation by dithiocarbamic acid following a concerted nucleophilic substitution at C(sp)‐center to produce Z‐styrenyl‐bis dithiocarbamates stereoselectively. Stereoselectivity arises due to neighboring‐group‐assisted addition of dithiocarbamic acid to the triple bond of alkynyl dithiocarbamate intermediate.
A series of bifunctionalized styrene molecules have been prepared by dithiocarbamylation of alkynyl bromides under catalyst and additive free condition. Freshly prepared dithiocarbamic acid reacts with alkynyl bromide under metal‐free condition following a concerted process to produce alkynyl‐dithiocarbamate intermediate as revealed from computational studies of the mechanism. Concerted nucleophilic substitution at C(sp)‐center occurs through a 3‐atom centered transition state. Neighboring‐group‐assisted addition of dithiocarbamic acid to the alkynyl‐dithiocarbamate intermediate furnishes stereoselectively Z‐styrenyl‐1,2‐bis dithiocarbamate compounds. The protocol is tested on gram‐scale synthesis and explored on substrates containing bioactive moieties.
item.content:encoded:
Alkynyl bromide undergoes metal-free activation by dithiocarbamic acid following a concerted nucleophilic substitution at C(sp)-center to produce Z-styrenyl-bis dithiocarbamates stereoselectively. Stereoselectivity arises due to neighboring-group-assisted addition of dithiocarbamic acid to the triple bond of alkynyl dithiocarbamate intermediate.


A series of bifunctionalized styrene molecules have been prepared by dithiocarbamylation of alkynyl bromides under catalyst and additive free condition. Freshly prepared dithiocarbamic acid reacts with alkynyl bromide under metal-free condition following a concerted process to produce alkynyl-dithiocarbamate intermediate as revealed from computational studies of the mechanism. Concerted nucleophilic substitution at C(sp)-center occurs through a 3-atom centered transition state. Neighboring-group-assisted addition of dithiocarbamic acid to the alkynyl-dithiocarbamate intermediate furnishes stereoselectively Z-styrenyl-1,2-bis dithiocarbamate compounds. The protocol is tested on gram-scale synthesis and explored on substrates containing bioactive moieties.
item.dc:creator: Sourav Mondal, Tanmay Naskar, Rakesh Kumar Roy, Soumya Mondal, Debajyoti Saha, Amit Saha
item.dc:title: Stereoselective Synthesis of Z‐Styrenyl‐1,2‐bis Dithiocarbamate via Metal‐Free Activation of Alkynyl Bromide

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Ammonium Iodide (NH4I)‐Mediated Transformations: Toward Greener and Metal‐Free Organic Synthesis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review offers a thorough examination of NH4I‐mediated thermal, photochemical, and electrochemical methodologies that have been reported from 2020 to the present. It emphasizes the key mechanistic features, reaction scopes, and synthetic applications. The objective is to encourage further innovation in eco‐friendly, sustainable, and energy‐efficient chemical transformations.
Ammonium iodide (NH4I) has recently emerged as a versatile and sustainable reagent in modern organic synthesis. This inexpensive and readily available inorganic salt serves as a redox mediator, promoter, and mild iodide source, enabling a broad spectrum of C–C, C–heteroatom, heteroatom–heteroatom, and one‐pot multibond‐forming reactions under environmentally benign conditions. Its dual functionality as both a nucleophilic iodide donor and an oxidative promoter has facilitated diverse transformations, including oxidative couplings, cyclizations, halogenations, and heterocyclic syntheses. This review provides a comprehensive overview of NH4I‐mediated thermal, photochemical, and electrochemical methodologies reported from 2020 to the present, emphasizing key mechanistic features, reaction scopes, and synthetic applications. By consolidating these recent advancements, this work underscores the growing importance of ammonium iodide as an efficient, green, and metal‐free mediator in contemporary organic synthesis and aims to inspire further innovation in sustainable, energy‐efficient, and environmentally responsible chemical transformations.
item.content:encoded:
This review offers a thorough examination of NH4I-mediated thermal, photochemical, and electrochemical methodologies that have been reported from 2020 to the present. It emphasizes the key mechanistic features, reaction scopes, and synthetic applications. The objective is to encourage further innovation in eco-friendly, sustainable, and energy-efficient chemical transformations.


Ammonium iodide (NH4I) has recently emerged as a versatile and sustainable reagent in modern organic synthesis. This inexpensive and readily available inorganic salt serves as a redox mediator, promoter, and mild iodide source, enabling a broad spectrum of C–C, C–heteroatom, heteroatom–heteroatom, and one-pot multibond-forming reactions under environmentally benign conditions. Its dual functionality as both a nucleophilic iodide donor and an oxidative promoter has facilitated diverse transformations, including oxidative couplings, cyclizations, halogenations, and heterocyclic syntheses. This review provides a comprehensive overview of NH4I-mediated thermal, photochemical, and electrochemical methodologies reported from 2020 to the present, emphasizing key mechanistic features, reaction scopes, and synthetic applications. By consolidating these recent advancements, this work underscores the growing importance of ammonium iodide as an efficient, green, and metal-free mediator in contemporary organic synthesis and aims to inspire further innovation in sustainable, energy-efficient, and environmentally responsible chemical transformations.
